query: constitutional growth delay diagnostic criteria including bone age, puberty markers, family history

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Short Stature -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Following a thorough history and relevant physical examination, most cases of familial short stature (FSS) can be properly diagnosed. Some experienced providers do not investigate at all when the diagnosis of FSS is clear. However, in certain instances, when CDGP occurs in combination with FSS, the provider is dealing with a very short child, much shorter than the usual FSS patient, and added to this is the additional delay in the onset of puberty. A family history of delayed puberty, in addition to SS in other family members, may be a good clue to this combination. Such clues may include a male relative with delayed facial hair growth and late age of onset of shaving or a female relative with delayed menarche. A bone age (BA) estimation using an x-ray of the non-dominant hand along with standard tables of ossification provides vital tools. The BA equals the chronological age (CA) in FSS.

retrieved_docs.title: Constitutional Growth Delay -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Any patient diagnosed with short stature should undergo further evaluation to determine if it's just constitutional growth delay or short familial statute. Further workup includes an endocrinology panel and bone age determination; this is done with an image of the left hand and wrist, most used methods to establish bone age are Greulich, Pyle atlas, and Tanner-Whitehouse 3 (TW3), this last one is believed to be the most accurate one. It’s important to remember that African Americans tend to have an advanced bone age and southeast Asians tend to have delayed bone age when compared to their peers.

retrieved_docs.title: Constitutional Growth Delay -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to remember that only about 3% of short stature patients have an actual endocrinologic problem that will need further treatment, which is why it is important to take a good history. If there is a concern in growth delay after proper height documentation, the patient needs to be sent for GH levels, IGF-1, and bone age. If this results within normal limits including a delay in bone age that correlates with the height of the patient, most likely it is safe to continue to monitor the patient. If taking this approach, and any of the laboratory workup or bone age are uncertain, or this type of lab workup cannot be done or properly interpreted, a specialist referral is in order. These are other recommendations on when to refer a patient [13] :

retrieved_docs.content: Growth parameters, including height, weight, and head circumference, should be plotted to determine the growth velocity.

retrieved_docs.title: Amenorrhea -- Evaluation -- Subsequent Diagnostic Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Adolescents with globally delayed puberty Bone age radiography Insulin-like growth factor I (IGF-1), to screen for growth hormone deficiency if growth velocity is low [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Constitutional Growth Delay -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: As far for lab workup, it should begin with a full endocrinologic panel that will include other causes of short stature, including TSH, T4, bone age, IG1, IGFBP3, karyotype (if suspected), bone age, even imaging like MRI can be a consideration, and at last, the stimulation GH test is done. To get to this point, the clinician will need low serum concentrations of GH, IGF-1, and IGFBP-3.

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Short Stature -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A recent study of children with FSS, CDGP, and idiopathic growth hormone deficiency (IGHD) showed a tendency for IGHD to have a progressive decrease in the height standard deviation scores (Ht SDS) throughout the first five years of life, whereas in FSS and CDGP it was seen mainly in the first two years . When serial heights/lengths are measured, this observation may help detect these conditions earlier. [17] [18] A nutritional evaluation will reveal a good appetite, appropriate weight gain, and correct weight for age, and the absence of significant deficiencies in FSS. A history of any use of medications like corticosteroids or other treatments that can interfere with growth, like radiation therapy for malignant disorders, should be obtained.

retrieved_docs.title: Constitutional Growth Delay -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: After having accurate measurements and a good mid-parental height, one proceeds actually to answer the question; is the child truly short? If the child is below the 2.3 percentile, the diagnosis of short stature is made; below the 1 percentile is extreme short stature; then proceed to determine the etiology.

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Short Stature -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A child with FSS will grow with a normal growth velocity, albeit in the lowest percentile. Such a child will also progress to puberty at the appropriate age range. In FSS, the final height is in the target height range for the family. In those children with combined FSS and CDGP, the SS will be more severe, brought to attention when puberty is delayed and will attain a final predicted height at a later age than their peers.

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Age -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Bone age assessment is a requirement in the following: Endocrine/metabolic/pediatric conditions [28] [7] [29] : Diagnosis and management of endocrine disorders Evaluation of metabolic growth disorders (tall/short stature) Deceleration of maturity in various syndromic disorders; and Assessment of treatment response in various developmental disorders

retrieved_docs.title: Constitutional Growth Delay -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: GH stimulation test is the closest to there is to a gold standard to diagnose constitutional growth delay through ruling out GH deficiency. This is true for idiopathic short stature diagnosis as well. Some limitations of the study important to remember are that, increase BMI influences GH response as well as recent steroid use.

retrieved_docs.title: Short Stature -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The GHST is considered when anthropometric criteria suggest possible GHD. To improve diagnostic specificity, the Pediatric Endocrine Society recommends sex steroid priming in boys older than 11 years and girls older than 10 years who have not reached at least Tanner stage 3. Without priming, children with CDGP or CDGP with FSS may be incorrectly diagnosed with GHD. Priming involves either an intramuscular injection of testosterone 100 mg in boys 5 to 7 days before testing or oral estradiol valerate 2 mg daily (1 mg for children <20 kg) for 3 days in both sexes. The same guidelines caution against relying solely on GHST results for diagnosis. Instead, these values should be interpreted in the context of the patient’s growth trajectory, clinical risk factors, and other investigations.

retrieved_docs.content: Family History : Were any biological siblings or parents considered "late bloomers" in their family?

retrieved_docs.title: Short Stature -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Radiologic studies include a left hand posteroanterior x-ray to determine bone age, with or without a complete skeletal survey (spine, long bones, hands, pelvis, and skull) if disproportion or skeletal dysplasia is suspected. Bone age is typically delayed in endocrine conditions and plays a key role in predicting adult height. Common methods for assessing bone age include the Greulich-Pyle, Tanner-Whitehouse, and automated BoneXpert systems. Bone age correlates more closely with pubertal development than chronological age and can support, but not confirm, a diagnosis such as FSS.

retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that children born prematurely with low birth weight have an increased risk of developing reduced height later in life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2821
retrieved_docs.content: Threat of premature labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر) is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that individuals who are shorter in height may have an increased risk of experiencing preterm labor. This association highlights the importance of recognizing short stature as a potential clinical indicator when assessing pregnant patients for the likelihood of premature delivery, thereby informing monitoring strategies and preventive care measures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often experience growth restriction that results in reduced adult height. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this context reflects severe underlying health challenges that increase the risk of death shortly after birth. Recognizing the link between low‑birth‑weight preterm infants, subsequent short stature, and heightened neonatal mortality can guide clinicians in early monitoring and intervention strategies to improve outcomes for this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Preterm low‑birth‑weight infants are clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature itself is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), indicating that children who remain short may have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Additionally, تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) is clinically associated with قصر القامة, suggesting that pregnancies complicated by early labor threats often result in offspring who develop short stature. Collectively, these relationships highlight short stature as a common clinical link among preterm low birth weight, threatened preterm labor, and adverse neonatal outcomes such as neonatal death, underscoring its relevance for risk assessment and early intervention in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2443
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as an extremely premature birth of unspecified weight.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 460
retrieved_docs.content: organs may manifest as uterine cyst (كيسة رحمية) and vaginal bleeding, highlighting diagnostic considerations. Short stature (قصر القامة) is clinically associated with neonatal death, indicating that individuals with short stature require careful perinatal monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 944
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal growth restriction (تقييد النمو الجنيني) is described as having a broader relationship with an unspecified condition that originated in the perinatal period (حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة), indicating that fetal growth restriction can be viewed as a more general clinical category under which certain undefined perinatal conditions may be classified, underscoring the importance of thorough assessment of growth abnormalities around the time of birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often develop reduced linear growth. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this vulnerable population may be linked to an increased risk of death shortly after birth. The Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology encompasses adverse pediatric outcomes, and its subset includes the concepts تدلّي الحبل السري (umbilical cord prolapse) and وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death). Together, these relationships highlight a network of interrelated risk factors and adverse events: preterm low birth weight, short stature, and neonatal death are closely connected, while umbilical cord prolapse is also recognized within the same adverse‑event framework, underscoring the importance of comprehensive monitoring and intervention in high‑risk newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2279
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone Agonist is classified as a hormone and related agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 362
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are associated with bone resorption, indicating that these conditions often involve increased breakdown of bone tissue, which contributes to skeletal weakening and structural abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1843
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation are classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني, which denotes fetal growth restriction. This condition is understood to be part of a broader, unspecified disorder that originates in the perinatal period, referred to as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Recognizing that تقييد النمو الجنيني falls within this larger perinatal context helps clinicians assess and manage infants presenting with these growth‑related complications.

Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.
